library.title: Top 50 Medicine Balls & Slam Balls Exercise Library
library.purpose: Build a phase-honest medicine-ball and slam-ball library for explosive throws, slams, trunk control, catch/deceleration skill, loaded ball strength, partner timing, and controlled repeatability.
library.sourceBasis: Primary source used: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md, the current Vortex Card v2 authoring guide. It defines phase-first authoring, canonical phase keys, taxonomy key+weight tags, publish gates, dosage, scaling cohorts, safety, regimen, phaseProfile, pairing logic, and the preferred camelCase Authoring JSON shape. Purpose.txt was requested and searched for by exact filename and related Purpose/Vortex/Athleticism Accelerator language, but it was not located in the accessible File Library results for this run. Therefore this library is grounded in exercise_card_details_for_llm.md, existing Vortex medicine-ball/pairing/SSC precedents surfaced in the File Library, the user's focus on medicine balls and slam balls, and stable athletic coaching expertise.
library.authoringFormat: Card v2 Authoring format: camelCase top-level card objects, canonical primaryPhaseKey, taxonomy key+weight tags, movementRequirements, coachingExecution, dosage, scaling, safety, regimen, pairingLogic, and phaseProfile.

categories.title: Foundational Medicine-Ball Throwing Power
categories.focus: High-intent, low-volume throws that develop upper-body, trunk, hip-to-hand, and rotational power with less lower-body impact than repeated jumps.

categories.title: Slam-Ball Total-Body Power and Neural Potentiation
categories.focus: Explosive dead-ball and rebound-ball patterns that train violent but organized force transfer, trunk stiffness, hip snap, and clean resets without conditioning drift.

categories.title: Trunk Control, Core Resilience, and Position Ownership
categories.focus: Medicine-ball drills used as load, counterbalance, or timing constraint to build bracing, anti-rotation, trunk compression, shoulder control, and controlled rotation.

categories.title: Catching, Deceleration, and Movement Intelligence
categories.focus: Catch-and-stick, target, tracking, and relocation drills that connect eyes, hands, trunk organization, footwork, braking, and safe reset behavior.

categories.title: Partner Integration, Loaded Capacity, and Late-Session Repeatability
categories.focus: Partner exchange, loaded slam-ball strength, carry, ground-to-shoulder, and controlled conditioning options where phase placement and quality gates prevent chaos.

cards.name: Medicine Ball Chest Pass
cards.category: Foundational Medicine-Ball Throwing Power
cards.categoryFocus: High-intent, low-volume throws that develop upper-body, trunk, hip-to-hand, and rotational power with less lower-body impact than repeated jumps.
cards.cardSummary: Medicine Ball Chest Pass is a medicine-ball exercise for horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, and fast chest-to-hand force transfer. Use it when that quality is the priority, not as random ball work or fatigue entertainment.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after Prepare & Access and any needed Movement Intelligence, before heavy strength, conditioning, or high-volume sport work. Keep volume low, intent high, and rest long enough for every throw, slam, or rebound to look sharp.
cards.description: Perform Medicine Ball Chest Pass with a medicine ball to train horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, and fast chest-to-hand force transfer. The load, distance, partner input, and volume should preserve the card's phase intent.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: jump_throw_explosive_power. Coach the ball as a constraint: choose the load and setup that make the athlete move better, faster, or with more control; stop before fatigue changes the pattern.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move the ball with intent, own your posture, and reset before the next rep.
cards.selectionRationale: Selected because it uses medicine/slam ball constraints to develop horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, and fast chest-to-hand force transfer with clear setup, phase honesty, and scalable safety gates.
cards.selectionTarget: horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, and fast chest-to-hand force transfer
cards.whyItWorks: The ball gives immediate feedback: speed, accuracy, rebound, catch quality, or load path changes when the athlete leaks force. Medicine Ball Chest Pass therefore links bracing, timing, and force transfer in a coachable way.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Output because the drill expresses high-quality speed, power, throwing intent, stiffness, or reactive athleticism while fresh. The slot `jump_throw_explosive_power` keeps it away from fatigue-based conditioning.
cards.commonMisuse: The common mistake is turning a power drill into conditioning by using too many reps, too heavy a ball, short rest, or counting slow throws after speed and posture have dropped.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing ball load, distance, rotation range, approach speed, or reactive demand before increasing volume. Progress only one variable at a time and let the quality gate decide.

implement_profile.load_rule: Light enough for speed and accuracy on Output cards; heavy enough for control only on Capacity cards; never choose load that changes the pattern.
implement_profile.surface_rule: Use wall/floor/partner setup that matches ball rebound behavior. Dead slam balls should not be expected to rebound.

output_profile.quality_rule: every rep must be fast, accurate, and controlled; stop when ball speed or posture drops

coachingExecution.movement_description: Medicine Ball Chest Pass uses the medicine ball to train horizontal upper-body power, trunk stiffness, and fast chest-to-hand force transfer. The drill is valuable only when the athlete can keep the intended phase quality, safe spacing, and repeatable mechanics.
setup: Choose a medicine ball that matches the drill intent and athlete size.
Use a solid wall target and stand far enough away that the ball returns safely, if it returns at all.
Clear the lane, wall, floor, or partner space before the first rep.
Explain the finish position and stop command before adding speed.
execution_steps: Load hips, trunk, and hands into the starting shape.
Throw the ball with maximal intent while keeping the torso organized.
Catch, recover, or collect the ball safely.
Reset posture before the next attempt.
common_faults: Choosing a ball too heavy for speed.
Leaking through the ribs or low back.
Over-rotating after release.
Counting slow, inaccurate throws as good reps.
quality_gate: Ball speed, catch quality, or load path stays consistent across the set.
Ribs, pelvis, and spine stay organized without pain.
Athlete can reset safely before the next rep or interval.
stop_signs: Pain, dizziness, breath-holding panic, or unsafe ball rebound.
Throw, slam, catch, or footwork quality drops for two consecutive reps.
Partner spacing, wall target, or floor conditions become unsafe.

dosage.notes: Stop the set when speed, ball velocity, accuracy, or trunk position drops. Do not chase fatigue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the lightest ball, slower tempo, short distance, and a simple finish. Prioritize safe handling and clear stop commands over power.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use light-to-moderate load and add one constraint at a time, such as distance, rotation, target, or partner timing.
scaling.teen: Progress load or speed only after posture, catch/throw accuracy, and reset behavior are repeatable across all sets.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with low volume and conservative load. Remove rebound, rotation, or locomotion if bracing or ball path is not reliable.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use higher intent, slightly heavier implements, longer throws, or more sport-specific angles while keeping the same quality gate.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer light medicine-ball control, chest-level throws, seated/tall-kneeling options, or low-speed carries. Avoid aggressive slams or unpredictable rebound unless clearly prepared.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical clearance when appropriate. Prefer seated, half-kneeling, or anti-rotation alternatives; avoid breath-holding, high-impact slams, aggressive trunk flexion, and supine work when not tolerated.
scaling.genderSpecificNotes: No default male/female loading split. Scale by size, training age, readiness, pain history, and ability to control the implement.

readiness_checks: Athlete can brace and breathe without pain.
Ball weight and rebound behavior are appropriate for the drill.
Space, wall, floor, and partner lane are clear before starting.
contraindications: Current back, shoulder, wrist, hip, knee, or abdominal pain aggravated by the pattern.
Inability to control the ball path or stop command.
Unsafe wall, floor, rebound, or partner spacing.

Internal coaching reference: medicine-ball and slam-ball load/rebound safety checklist
mediaInternalNotes: Film from front and side. Approve progression only when ball speed, posture, catch quality, and spacing stay repeatable.

cards.selectionTarget: total-body downward power, trunk stiffness, and safe force expression into the floor
cards.whyItWorks: The ball gives immediate feedback: speed, accuracy, rebound, catch quality, or load path changes when the athlete leaks force. Slam Ball Overhead Slam therefore links bracing, timing, and force transfer in a coachable way.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Output because the drill expresses high-quality speed, power, throwing intent, stiffness, or reactive athleticism while fresh. The slot `jump_throw_explosive_power` keeps it away from fatigue-based conditioning.
cards.commonMisuse: The common mistake is turning a power drill into conditioning by using too many reps, too heavy a ball, short rest, or counting slow throws after speed and posture have dropped.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing ball load, distance, rotation range, approach speed, or reactive demand before increasing volume. Progress only one variable at a time and let the quality gate decide.

implement_profile.load_rule: Light enough for speed and accuracy on Output cards; heavy enough for control only on Capacity cards; never choose load that changes the pattern.
implement_profile.surface_rule: Use wall/floor/partner setup that matches ball rebound behavior. Dead slam balls should not be expected to rebound.

output_profile.quality_rule: every rep must be fast, accurate, and controlled; stop when ball speed or posture drops

coachingExecution.movement_description: Slam Ball Overhead Slam uses the slam ball to train total-body downward power, trunk stiffness, and safe force expression into the floor. The drill is valuable only when the athlete can keep the intended phase quality, safe spacing, and repeatable mechanics.
setup: Choose a slam ball that matches the drill intent and athlete size.
Clear the lane, wall, floor, or partner space before the first rep.
Explain the finish position and stop command before adding speed.
execution_steps: Set the feet, ribs, and hips before lifting the ball.
Drive the ball hard into the floor without losing trunk position.
Recover the ball with a hinge or squat pattern instead of rounding the back.
Reset fully before the next rep unless this card is explicitly being used as late-session conditioning.
common_faults: Using a ball that is too heavy to move fast.
Arching overhead or rounding during pickup.
Turning quality reps into a fatigue race.
Letting the ball bounce unpredictably into the face or partner space.
quality_gate: Ball speed, catch quality, or load path stays consistent across the set.
Ribs, pelvis, and spine stay organized without pain.
Athlete can reset safely before the next rep or interval.
stop_signs: Pain, dizziness, breath-holding panic, or unsafe ball rebound.
Throw, slam, catch, or footwork quality drops for two consecutive reps.
Partner spacing, wall target, or floor conditions become unsafe.

dosage.notes: Stop the set when speed, ball velocity, accuracy, or trunk position drops. Do not chase fatigue.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the lightest ball, slower tempo, short distance, and a simple finish. Prioritize safe handling and clear stop commands over power.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use light-to-moderate load and add one constraint at a time, such as distance, rotation, target, or partner timing.
scaling.teen: Progress load or speed only after posture, catch/throw accuracy, and reset behavior are repeatable across all sets.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with low volume and conservative load. Remove rebound, rotation, or locomotion if bracing or ball path is not reliable.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use higher intent, slightly heavier implements, longer throws, or more sport-specific angles while keeping the same quality gate.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer light medicine-ball control, chest-level throws, seated/tall-kneeling options, or low-speed carries. Avoid aggressive slams or unpredictable rebound unless clearly prepared.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical clearance when appropriate. Prefer seated, half-kneeling, or anti-rotation alternatives; avoid breath-holding, high-impact slams, aggressive trunk flexion, and supine work when not tolerated.
scaling.genderSpecificNotes: No default male/female loading split. Scale by size, training age, readiness, pain history, and ability to control the implement.

readiness_checks: Athlete can brace and breathe without pain.
Ball weight and rebound behavior are appropriate for the drill.
Space, wall, floor, and partner lane are clear before starting.
contraindications: Current back, shoulder, wrist, hip, knee, or abdominal pain aggravated by the pattern.
Inability to control the ball path or stop command.
Unsafe wall, floor, rebound, or partner spacing.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Medicine Ball Plank Pull-Through uses the medicine ball to train shoulder support and anti-rotation while the ball moves across the midline. The drill is valuable only when the athlete can keep the intended phase quality, safe spacing, and repeatable mechanics.
setup: Choose a medicine ball that matches the drill intent and athlete size.
Clear the lane, wall, floor, or partner space before the first rep.
Explain the finish position and stop command before adding speed.
execution_steps: Set ribs, pelvis, and ball position before moving.
Move the ball or body slowly enough to keep the trunk quiet.
Exhale through the hardest part of the rep.
End the set when the athlete loses alignment or breath control.
coach_cues: Move the ball, not the spine.
common_faults: Using momentum instead of trunk control.
Holding breath or flaring the ribs.
Moving into pain or low-back extension.
Choosing a ball too heavy for the position.
breathing_cues: Slow nasal inhale before the rep.
Do not hold the breath to survive the set.
quality_gate: Ball speed, catch quality, or load path stays consistent across the set.
Ribs, pelvis, and spine stay organized without pain.
Athlete can reset safely before the next rep or interval.
stop_signs: Pain, dizziness, breath-holding panic, or unsafe ball rebound.
Throw, slam, catch, or footwork quality drops for two consecutive reps.
Partner spacing, wall target, or floor conditions become unsafe.

dosage.notes: Keep tempo, bracing, and catch/control quality repeatable. End before compensation.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use the lightest ball, slower tempo, short distance, and a simple finish. Prioritize safe handling and clear stop commands over power.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use light-to-moderate load and add one constraint at a time, such as distance, rotation, target, or partner timing.
scaling.teen: Progress load or speed only after posture, catch/throw accuracy, and reset behavior are repeatable across all sets.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with low volume and conservative load. Remove rebound, rotation, or locomotion if bracing or ball path is not reliable.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use higher intent, slightly heavier implements, longer throws, or more sport-specific angles while keeping the same quality gate.
scaling.older_adult: Prefer light medicine-ball control, chest-level throws, seated/tall-kneeling options, or low-speed carries. Avoid aggressive slams or unpredictable rebound unless clearly prepared.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use medical clearance when appropriate. Prefer seated, half-kneeling, or anti-rotation alternatives; avoid breath-holding, high-impact slams, aggressive trunk flexion, and supine work when not tolerated.
